content:
6 min read
Just now

--

A practical comparison of two leading secret scanners, and the npm-native alternative that requires zero setup.
Hardcoded secrets are one of the most common and costly security mistakes in software development. An AWS key accidentally committed to a public repo, a Stripe live secret in a test fixture, a database URL with credentials embedded — these show up in post-mortems constantly. Bots actively scrape GitHub for leaked keys and the window between a push and exploitation is often under five minutes.
The tooling to prevent this has matured significantly. Two tools dominate the conversation: Gitleaks and TruffleHog. Both are production-grade, widely used, and actively maintained. But if you work primarily in Node.js and npm, there is a gap neither tool fills cleanly.

GitLeaks
Gitleaks is an open source secret scanner written in Go. It has 17k+ GitHub stars, 150+ built-in detectors, and is one of the most widely recommended tools for scanning repos before or after a push.
Setup
# macOS
brew install gitleaks# Linux / Windows — download binary from GitHub releases
# https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks/releases
# Verify
gitleaks version
Basic Usage
# Scan current directory
gitleaks detect --source . -v# Scan full git history
gitleaks detect --source . --log-opts="--all" -v
# Output as SARIF (for GitHub Code Scanning)
gitleaks detect --source . --report-format sarif --report-path results.sarif
What Gitleaks does well
Speed: Gitleaks is fast on large repos. It uses Go’s concurrency and runs pattern matching in parallel. On a monorepo with 50k files it finishes in seconds.
Configuration: You can define custom rules in a `.gitleaks.toml` file, add allowlists for known false positives, and tune per-path behavior without touching source code.
GitHub Actions integration: There is an official Gitleaks GitHub Action that makes CI setup straightforward:
- name: Gitleaks scan
uses: gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2
env:
GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}Allowlists: When a finding is a known false positive, Gitleaks lets you suppress it inline with a comment or via config, without suppressing the entire rule.
Where Gitleaks falls short for Node.js developers
Go dependency in a JavaScript project. Adding Gitleaks to a Node.js CI pipeline means installing Go or downloading a platform-specific binary. For a team that only works in JavaScript, this is an unexpected context switch — especially when onboarding new developers who ask why the project has a Go binary in its workflow.
No programmatic API. Gitleaks is CLI-only. If you want to embed secret scanning in a custom script, a test pipeline, or an editor plugin, you are shelling out to a subprocess and parsing stdout. There is no import { scan } from ‘gitleaks’.
npm ecosystem friction. Running npx eslint or npx tsc is second nature for Node.js developers. Gitleaks does not fit that pattern — it requires a separate installation step that breaks the flow.

TruffleHog
TruffleHog, maintained by Truffle Security, takes a fundamentally different approach. Where Gitleaks detects patterns, TruffleHog can verify whether a detected secret is still active by actually calling the API.
Setup
# macOS
brew install trufflehog# via pip
pip install trufflehog
# via Docker
docker run --rm -it -v "$PWD:/pwd" \
trufflesecurity/trufflehog:latest \
git file:///pwd --only-verified
Basic Usage
# Scan local repo — verified secrets only
trufflehog git file://. --only-verified# Scan a GitHub repo
trufflehog github --repo https://github.com/org/repo
# Scan an S3 bucket
trufflehog s3 --bucket my-bucket
What TruffleHog does well
Verified detection. This is TruffleHog’s defining feature. For each finding it makes a live API call to confirm the credential is still active. The difference between a rotated key and a live one determines whether you have an incident or a cleanup task. No other open source scanner does this.
700+ detectors. TruffleHog covers almost every SaaS tool in existence — not just the obvious ones like AWS and GitHub, but Shopify, Twilio, Sendbird, Buildkite, dozens of others. The coverage is comprehensive.
Scans beyond local repos. TruffleHog can scan GitHub organizations, GitLab groups, S3 buckets, GCS buckets, Slack workspaces, Jira, and Confluence. For security teams doing broad audits, this is significant.
Active development. New detectors are added regularly and the maintainers are responsive. The project has strong momentum.
Where TruffleHog falls short for Node.js developers
Python or Docker required. For a JavaScript developer the setup path runs through pip or Docker. Neither is complex, but both require stepping outside the Node.js toolchain entirely.
No programmatic API for Node.js. Like Gitleaks, TruffleHog is CLI-only. Embedding it in custom Node.js tooling requires subprocess calls.
Overhead for single-repo use. TruffleHog is built for scale — scanning entire GitHub organizations, multiple cloud buckets. For a single Node.js repo where you want a pre-commit hook and a CI check, it is significantly more tool than the job requires.

The gap both tools leave
After evaluating both, a pattern becomes clear: Gitleaks and TruffleHog are built for DevOps engineers and security teams who are comfortable with Go binaries, Docker containers, and Python environments. Both are excellent at what they were designed for.
What neither addresses is the Node.js developer who wants to run a secret scan the same way they run any other tool in their project — with npx, zero installation, no new toolchain, and ideally a JavaScript API they can import.
The equivalent in the logging space: Winston and Pino both solve logging, but Pino’s positioning around performance and its clean API made it the default for new Node.js projects. The tool that fits the ecosystem wins the ecosystem.

secretguard
secretguard is a secret scanner built for Node.js developers. Zero config, zero installation beyond Node.js itself.
npx secretguard
Scans the current directory, outputs findings grouped by severity, and exits with code 1 if CRITICAL findings are detected — making it a drop-in CI gate without any setup.
Pattern coverage (46 detectors)
- AWS, OpenAI, Anthropic, GitHub, GitLab, Slack, Stripe, Twilio
- HuggingFace, Vercel, Supabase, Cloudflare, Azure, Firebase
- PyPI, Doppler, SendGrid, npm access tokens
- PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB URLs with embedded credentials
- RSA, EC, OpenSSH, and PGP private keys
- PII — SSN, credit cards, email, phone (automatically skipped in test and fixture files to reduce false positives)
Git history scanning
secretguard . --history
secretguard . --history --jsonPre-commit hook — one command
secretguard install-hookWrites a pre-commit hook to .git/hooks that runs secretguard — staged before every commit. Blocks on CRITICAL findings. No manual hook file editing required.
GitHub Code Scanning via SARIF
GitHub’s Security tab and PR annotations are normally gated behind GitHub Advanced Security at $49/user/month. Uploading SARIF output from any tool bypasses this — you get the same UI for free.
# .github/workflows/secretguard.yml
name: Secret scan
on: [push, pull_request]jobs:
scan:
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
- name: Scan for secrets
run: npx secretguard . --sarif results.sarif
- name: Upload to GitHub Security tab
uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v3
with:
sarif_file: results.sarif
Baseline suppression
# Accept current findings as known baseline
secretguard . --baseline .secretguard-baseline.json --update-baseline# CI only surfaces new findings going forward
secretguard . --baseline .secretguard-baseline.json
Programmatic API
import { scan } from 'secretguard'
import type { ScanResult } from 'secretguard'const result: ScanResult = await scan('./src', {
ignore: ['tests', 'fixtures'],
})
console.log(result.findings) // Finding[]
console.log(result.scanned) // number of files scanned
console.log(result.duration) // ms
This is the feature Gitleaks and TruffleHog do not have. If you are building a custom CI tool, an editor extension, or any automation around secret detection, this is the surface you need.
Side-by-side comparison
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Which one should you use?
TruffleHog is the right choice when verified detection matters — knowing whether a leaked key is still active changes your response from cleanup to incident. It is also the best option for scanning beyond your local repo: GitHub organizations, S3 buckets, Slack workspaces. If you are a security engineer running broad audits, TruffleHog is the most powerful tool available.
Gitleaks fits best in polyglot environments where Go tooling is already present, or when you need fine-grained custom rules via .gitleaks.toml. It is fast, reliable, and trusted by security teams at scale.
secretguard fits best in Node.js and TypeScript projects where the team works entirely in the npm ecosystem. If npx is how your team runs tools, if you need a programmatic API for custom integrations, or if you want GitHub Code Scanning annotations on pull requests without paying for GitHub Advanced Security — secretguard covers all of that with zero toolchain overhead.
The practical approach for most teams: use secretguard in local development and pre-commit hooks for fast, zero-friction feedback, and add TruffleHog as a scheduled CI scan for verified detection across the full history. They complement each other rather than compete.
